summary: The previous approach registered rollover flags and used them to generate ena pulses, but this likely introduced a one-cycle misalignment between the BCD digit increment and the ena pulse. To materially change strategy, we now generate ena pulses combinationally, directly in the same clock cycle as the digit increments, by detecting when a digit will roll over on the next clock. This ensures ena is asserted exactly when the corresponding digit increments, matching the reference model's timing. The ena outputs are now assigned based on the current state of the digits and the increment conditions, not on delayed rollover flags.
rtl_lines: // ena[0]: pulse high when ones digit rolls over (i.e., about to increment tens)
// ena[1]: pulse high when tens digit rolls over (i.e., about to increment hundreds)
// ena[2]: pulse high when hundreds digit rolls over (i.e., about to increment thousands)
// Output q as concatenation of BCD digits
risks: If the reference model expects ena to be registered (i.e., pulse appears one cycle after the rollover), this approach may still mismatch. However, the evidence suggests the previous registered approach was off by one cycle.
If the testbench samples ena at a different phase than the combinational assignment, there could be a race, but the testbench appears to sample on clock edges.
next_steps: Run simulation and compare ena outputs to the reference model, especially at rollover points.
If mismatches persist, probe the exact timing of ena pulses relative to digit increments and reference outputs.
If needed, try a hybrid approach: register ena outputs but drive them from the combinational rollover conditions.
